docs.title: Hiero Enterprise Java — Technical Documentation
docs.text:
Welcome to the technical documentation for Hiero Enterprise Java.

This site contains:

  • Getting started — How to add and configure Hiero Enterprise in your project.
  • Architecture — High-level structure and components.
  • Spring Boot — Spring Boot integration and usage.
  • MicroProfile — MicroProfile / Quarkus integration and usage.
  • Base / Managed services — Core clients and services (AccountClient, FileClient, etc.).

Hiero Enterprise Java provides Java modules to interact with a Hiero network in Spring Boot or Eclipse MicroProfile (e.g. Quarkus) applications.

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
• GitHub repository
  • README / user guide

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Hiero Enterprise Java is split into three main modules:

  • hiero-enterprise-base contains the shared core APIs and implementations.
  • hiero-enterprise-spring integrates the base module with Spring Boot.
  • hiero-enterprise-microprofile integrates the base module with Eclipse MicroProfile implementations such as Quarkus.

docs.title: Base module
docs.text:
The base module contains the core service contracts and their shared implementations. This includes:

  • protocol-layer clients for submitting transactions
  • mirror-node clients and repositories for query use cases
  • shared data models such as accounts, tokens, NFTs, topics, contracts, and pages
  • configuration abstractions such as HieroConfig and HieroContext

This module is the foundation used by both framework integrations.

docs.title: Spring Boot module
docs.text:
The Spring Boot module provides:

  • the @EnableHiero annotation
  • Spring Boot configuration properties under spring.hiero.*
  • Spring-managed beans for the managed services defined in the base module
  • Spring-specific mirror-node REST and JSON converter implementations

Applications using Spring Boot depend on this module instead of wiring the base services manually.

docs.title: MicroProfile module
docs.text:
The MicroProfile module provides:

  • CDI producers for the managed services
  • MicroProfile configuration classes under hiero.*
  • MicroProfile-specific mirror-node REST and JSON converter implementations

Applications using MicroProfile or Quarkus can inject the same service interfaces that are available in the base module.

docs.title: Main layers
docs.text:
The project is organized around two main interaction paths:

  1. Protocol-layer access for transactions and network operations
  2. Mirror-node access for read-only queries

In practice, applications typically work with high-level clients and repositories instead of using the lower-level SDK directly.

docs.title: Interaction flow
docs.text:
At a high level, the flow looks like this:

  1. Framework-specific configuration creates a HieroConfig
  2. HieroConfig creates a HieroContext
  3. The framework module exposes managed clients and repositories
  4. Transaction use cases go through the protocol-layer client
  5. Query use cases go through the mirror-node client and repositories

docs.title: External dependencies
docs.text: The project currently builds on top of the Hedera Java SDK and is being migrated to the Hiero namespace over time. Mirror-node queries are handled through REST-based clients exposed by the framework modules.

docs.title: Where to continue
docs.text:
• See Getting Started for basic setup.
  • See Spring Boot for Spring configuration and usage.
  • See MicroProfile for CDI and MicroProfile configuration.
  • See Base / Managed Services for the main services exposed to applications.

docs.title: Contributing to the docs
docs.text: This folder contains the source for the Hiero Enterprise Java technical documentation (GitHub Pages).

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
• index.md — Landing page and entry point.
  • getting-started.md — Getting started guide.
  • architecture.md — High-level architecture overview.
  • spring-boot.md — Spring Boot integration.
  • microprofile.md — MicroProfile integration.
  • managed-services.md — Base module and managed services.

The site is built with MkDocs and the Material for MkDocs theme. Configuration is in the repository root: mkdocs.yml.

docs.title: Building locally
docs.text:
From the repository root:

pip install mkdocs-material "pymdown-extensions"
mkdocs serve

Then open http://127.0.0.1:8000 .

docs.title: Publishing
docs.text:
The .github/workflows/docs.yml workflow:

  • Push to main: Builds and deploys the site to the production URL (root).
  • Pull request: Builds and deploys a preview to /pr/<number>/ and comments the preview URL on the PR.

Ensure GitHub Pages is enabled and set the source to Deploy from a branch → branch: gh-pages, folder: / (root).

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This guide shows the basic setup needed to use Hiero Enterprise Java in an application.

docs.title: Choose an integration module
docs.text:
Use one of the framework modules depending on your application:

  • hiero-enterprise-spring for Spring Boot
  • hiero-enterprise-microprofile for MicroProfile or Quarkus

The base module provides the shared APIs and implementations, but most applications should start with one of the framework integrations.

docs.title: Spring Boot dependency
docs.text:
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.hiero</groupId>
    <artifactId>hiero-enterprise-spring</artifactId>
    <version>VERSION</version>
</dependency>

docs.title: MicroProfile dependency
docs.text:
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.hiero</groupId>
    <artifactId>hiero-enterprise-microprofile</artifactId>
    <version>VERSION</version>
</dependency>

docs.title: Required configuration
docs.text:
In both integrations, you need to configure:

  • an operator account ID
  • the private key for that operator account
  • the target network or custom node settings

The project currently supports DER-encoded ECDSA private keys for the operator configuration.

docs.title: Common setup steps
docs.text:
1. Add the dependency for your framework
  2. Configure the operator account and network
  3. Enable or produce the Hiero services
  4. Inject a managed client such as AccountClient, FileClient, or SmartContractClient

docs.title: Network choices
docs.text: You can use one of the predefined Hedera-based networks or provide custom nodes and a mirror node endpoint. This is useful for local or custom environments such as Solo-based setups.

docs.title: Running the build
docs.text:
The project uses Maven and includes the Maven wrapper:

./mvnw verify

Some tests require real network credentials. If no account is configured, those tests will fail.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• See Spring Boot for Spring-specific setup.
  • See MicroProfile for MicroProfile-specific setup.
  • See Base / Managed Services for the available clients and repositories.

docs.title: Base / Managed Services
docs.text: The base module defines the main service interfaces used by both the Spring Boot and MicroProfile integrations.

docs.title: Core configuration objects
docs.text:
• HieroConfig creates framework-independent configuration
  • HieroContext holds the active network and operator account information

These objects are used internally by the framework integrations and shared implementations.

docs.title: Transaction-oriented clients
docs.text:
The following managed clients are used for write or transaction-based operations:

  • AccountClient
  • FileClient
  • FungibleTokenClient
  • NftClient
  • SmartContractClient
  • TopicClient
  • HookClient

These clients build on the protocol-layer support in the base module.

docs.title: Contract verification
docs.text: • ContractVerificationClient provides smart-contract verification support

docs.title: Mirror-node access
docs.text:
The base module also exposes a lower-level MirrorNodeClient plus repository-style abstractions for query use cases.

Available repository interfaces include:

  • AccountRepository
  • BlockRepository
  • ContractRepository
  • NetworkRepository
  • NftRepository
  • TokenRepository
  • TopicRepository
  • TransactionRepository

These repositories are intended for read-only access to data exposed through the mirror node.

docs.title: Protocol-layer access
docs.text:
• ProtocolLayerClient provides lower-level access for transaction submission and related protocol operations

Most applications should prefer the higher-level managed clients unless they need a lower-level integration point.

docs.title: Framework availability
docs.text:
The services in this module are exposed through:

  • Spring beans in hiero-enterprise-spring
  • CDI producers in hiero-enterprise-microprofile

That means application code can usually depend on the shared interfaces while letting the framework module provide the implementation.

docs.title: MicroProfile Integration
docs.text: The hiero-enterprise-microprofile module provides CDI-based integration for Eclipse MicroProfile runtimes such as Quarkus.

docs.title: Dependency
docs.text:
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.hiero</groupId>
    <artifactId>hiero-enterprise-microprofile</artifactId>
    <version>VERSION</version>
</dependency>

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
The MicroProfile integration reads configuration from the hiero.* namespace.

Example:

hiero.accountId=0.0.53854625
hiero.privateKey=YOUR_DER_ENCODED_PRIVATE_KEY
hiero.network.name=hedera-testnet

Additional network-related settings include:

  • hiero.network.nodes
  • hiero.network.mirrornode
  • hiero.network.requestTimeoutInMs

When hiero.network.nodes is provided, the named network setting is not used.

docs.title: Managed services
docs.text:
The module uses CDI producers to expose the same service interfaces defined in the base module.

Examples include:

  • AccountClient
  • FileClient
  • FungibleTokenClient
  • NftClient
  • SmartContractClient
  • TopicClient
  • MirrorNodeClient
  • repository interfaces such as AccountRepository, TokenRepository, and NftRepository

docs.title: Injection example
docs.text:
@ApplicationScoped
public class HieroAccountService {

    @Inject
    AccountClient accountClient;
}

docs.title: What the module provides
docs.text:
The MicroProfile module wires:

  • configuration objects for operator and network settings
  • a shared HieroConfig and HieroContext
  • CDI-produced protocol-layer clients
  • CDI-produced mirror-node clients and repositories
  • MicroProfile-specific REST and JSON converter implementations

docs.title: Sample application
docs.text: See the MicroProfile sample module in the repository for a minimal example using this integration.

docs.title: Spring Boot Integration
docs.text: The hiero-enterprise-spring module provides Spring Boot integration for the managed Hiero services.

docs.title: Dependency
docs.text:
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.hiero</groupId>
    <artifactId>hiero-enterprise-spring</artifactId>
    <version>VERSION</version>
</dependency>

docs.title: Enable Hiero support
docs.text:
Add @EnableHiero to your Spring Boot application:

import org.hiero.spring.EnableHiero;

@SpringBootApplication
@EnableHiero
public class Application {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SpringApplication.run(Application.class, args);
    }
}

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
The Spring integration uses properties under spring.hiero.*.

Example:

spring.hiero.accountId=0.0.53854625
spring.hiero.privateKey=YOUR_DER_ENCODED_PRIVATE_KEY
spring.hiero.network.name=hedera-testnet

Important properties:

  • spring.hiero.accountId
  • spring.hiero.privateKey
  • spring.hiero.network.name
  • spring.hiero.network.mirrorNode
  • spring.hiero.network.requestTimeoutInMs

You can also provide custom nodes through spring.hiero.network.nodes. When custom nodes are provided, the named network is ignored.

docs.title: Using managed services
docs.text:
Once Hiero support is enabled, the main services can be injected as Spring beans.

Example:

@Service
public class HieroFileService {

    private final FileClient fileClient;

    public HieroFileService(FileClient fileClient) {
        this.fileClient = fileClient;
    }

    public String readFile(FileId fileId) {
        return new String(fileClient.readFile(fileId));
    }
}

docs.title: What the module provides
docs.text:
The Spring module wires:

  • the Hiero configuration and context
  • protocol-layer clients
  • mirror-node clients and repositories
  • Spring-specific REST and JSON converter implementations

docs.title: Sample application
docs.text: See the Spring sample module in the repository for a minimal example application using this integration.

docs.title: Testing GitHub Actions using Forks
docs.text:
When developing GitHub Actions or automation bots for Enterprise-Java, it is risky to test directly on the main repository. A mistake could accidentally close valid Pull Requests, spam developers with comments, or break build pipelines.

To test safely, developers should use their personal fork of the repository. This guide explains how to set up a fork to mimic the main repository and test workflows effectively.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
By default, GitHub Actions are often disabled on forks to save resources. You must enable them manually:

  1. Go to your fork on GitHub (e.g., github.com/<your-username>/hiero-enterprise-java).
  2. Click on the Settings tab.
  3. On the left sidebar, click Actions > General.
  4. Select Allow all actions and reusable workflows.
  5. Click Save.

docs.title: The Testing Workflow
docs.text: Testing a bot or action usually involves two distinct parts: 1. The Logic: The code that runs the bot (e.g., the .sh script or .yml workflow). 2. The Trigger: An event that causes the bot to run (e.g., a new PR, a comment, or a specific time of day).

docs.title: Step 1: Update your Fork's Main Branch
docs.text:
GitHub Actions usually define the "active" workflows based on the files present in the default branch (usually main). To test your new bot logic:

  1. Create a branch with your changes (e.g., feat/new-bot-logic).
  2. Push this branch to your fork.
  3. Open a Pull Request targeting your fork's main branch (Base: <your-username>:main ← Compare: <your-username>:feat/new-bot-logic).
  4. Merge this PR into your fork's main.

Note: Your fork's main branch now acts as the "production" environment for your test. It contains the code that runs the tests.

docs.title: Step 2: Create a Test Scenario
docs.text:
Now that your fork has the updated logic, you need to create an event to trigger it.

  1. Create a new dummy branch (e.g., test/trigger-bot).
  2. Make the necessary changes to trigger the specific action (see examples below).
  3. Open a Pull Request within your fork (Base: <your-username>:main ← Compare: <your-username>:test/trigger-bot).

docs.title: Modifying Test Timescales
docs.text: Real-world conditions (like "21 days of inactivity") are impractical for testing. You should temporarily modify the code in your feature branch (Step 1) to simulate these conditions immediately.

docs.title: 1. Shortening Time Thresholds
docs.text:
If a bot waits for 21 days, change the variable to 0 days or minutes in your script.

Before (Production Code): 

# Wait for 21 days before marking as stale
DAYS="${DAYS:-21}"

After (Testing Code): 

# Set to 0 to treat everything as immediately stale for testing
DAYS="${DAYS:-0}"

docs.title: 2. accelerating Cron Schedules
docs.text:
If a workflow runs once a day, you don't want to wait 24 hours. Modify the .yml file to run frequently or allow manual triggers.

Before: 

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 12 * * *"  # Runs at 12:00 PM daily

After: 

on:
  workflow_dispatch:      # Allows manual button click in Actions tab
  schedule:
    - cron: "*/5 * * * *" # Runs every 5 minutes

docs.title: Example 1: Testing an Unverified Commit Bot
docs.text:
Goal: Ensure the bot posts a warning comment if a PR contains unsigned commits.

  1. Deploy Logic: Merge the workflow file that checks for GPG signatures into your fork's main.
  2. Trigger Scenario:
    • Create a new branch: git checkout -b test/unsigned-commit
    • Make a dummy change.
    • Commit without your GPG signature using the --no-gpg-sign flag: 
      git commit -m "test: unsigned commit" --no-gpg-sign
    • Push and open a PR to your fork's main.
  3. Verify: Wait a moment and check the PR timeline. The bot should post a comment or fail the check indicating the commit is unverified.

docs.title: Example 2: Testing an Inactivity Bot
docs.text:
Goal: Verify the bot unassigns users after a period of inactivity.

  1. Modify Logic: In your script (Step 1), set DAYS=0 so the bot considers any issue created "now" as stale.
  2. Deploy: Merge this change to your fork's main.
  3. Trigger Scenario:
    • Create a dummy Issue in your fork.
    • Assign yourself to the Issue.
    • Wait for the scheduled workflow to run (e.g., the 5-minute cron you set up) or manually trigger it via the Actions tab.
  4. Verify: Check if the bot posted a comment on the issue and removed you from the assignee list.

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
Once you have verified the functionality works as expected:

  1. Delete your test branches (test/trigger-bot, etc.).
  2. Close any dummy Pull Requests and Issues in your fork.
  3. Revert the timescale changes in your feature branch (e.g., change DAYS=0 back to DAYS=21, remove the */5 cron).
  4. Create a final Pull Request from your clean feature branch to the official upstream repository.
